source.title: Integrating continuous hypermutation with high‐throughput screening for optimization of cis,cis ‐muconic acid production in yeast

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The study aims to optimize cis,cis-muconic acid (CCM) production in yeast by evolving the rate-limiting PCA decarboxylase enzyme. Traditional directed evolution is labor-intensive and limited by screening throughput. The hypothesis is that combining continuous hypermutation via OrthoRep with high-throughput biosensor-based FACS can rapidly evolve metabolic enzymes without growth coupling. The initial problem is PCA accumulation due to suboptimal PCA decarboxylase activity, limiting CCM titers.

section_gists.heading: Summary
section_gists.gist: Directed evolution is powerful but labor-intensive. This study integrates OrthoRep for continuous mutagenesis with a transcription factor-based biosensor for high-throughput screening to evolve CCM pathway enzymes. After two weeks of iterative passaging and FACS, PCA decarboxylase variants improved CCM titers >13-fold. The workflow debottlenecks directed evolution of metabolic enzymes.
process_signals: Integration of OrthoRep and biosensor-based FACS
Iterative cycles of mutagenesis and screening
>13-fold improvement in CCM titer

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: Industrial biotechnology requires extensive engineering of microbial cell factories. Rational engineering is limited by cellular complexity. Directed evolution is an alternative but often time- and cost-intensive due to library cloning and transformation. In vivo evolution systems like OrthoRep enable continuous mutagenesis without iterative cloning. However, coupling production to growth is often not possible. Biosensors based on allosteric transcription factors (aTFs) can link pathway flux to fluorescence, enabling FACS-based screening. The CCM pathway has a rate-limiting step at PCA decarboxylase, causing PCA accumulation. No prior directed evolution of PCA decarboxylase has been reported. This study combines OrthoRep and a CCM biosensor to evolve PCA decarboxylase subunits.
process_signals: Problem: PCA accumulation limits CCM production
Constraint: Traditional directed evolution is labor-intensive
Hypothesis: OrthoRep + biosensor can rapidly evolve metabolic enzymes
No prior directed evolution of PCA decarboxylase
failures_or_limits: Traditional directed evolution requires iterative cloning and transformation
Growth coupling often not possible for metabolic engineering

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: Results section heading only; no content.

section_gists.heading: Parental strain design
section_gists.gist: The parental strain Sc-105 was engineered with the CCM pathway (PaAroZ, KpAroY.B, KpAroY.Ciso, KpAroY.D, CaCatA) and a BenM-based biosensor. OrthoRep system was installed with TP-DNAP1-4-2 on a nuclear plasmid and the AroY.B and AroY.Ciso genes on the p1 linear cytoplasmic chromosome. The strain also had Tkl1 overexpression to enhance precursor supply. The design allows continuous mutagenesis of the PCA decarboxylase subunits and fluorescence-based selection.
process_signals: Strain Sc-105 constructed with all necessary components
OrthoRep targets AroY.B and AroY.Ciso on p1 plasmid
Biosensor BenM for CCM detection

section_gists.heading: Selection of hypermutated CCM biosynthetic pathway enzymes
section_gists.gist: The parental strain Sc-105 was subjected to five rounds of OrthoRep mutagenesis and FACS selection. Each round: culture in Delft minimal medium, sort 1 million events, collect top 2% (10,000 cells), recover 24h in synthetic complete medium, then next round. A control experiment with 15 bulk passages without selection was run in parallel. Mutation rate estimated at ~1 mutation per 5 generations, with ~1 additional mutation per sort round. Selection schedule designed based on mutation rate, gene length, and generation time.
process_signals: Five iterative rounds of mutagenesis and FACS
Selection gate: top 2% fluorescence
Control: 15 bulk passages without selection
Mutation rate estimation guided experimental design
metrics: Mutation rate: ~1e-4 substitutions per base per cell

section_gists.heading: Characteristics and validation of selected enzyme variants
section_gists.gist: Population fluorescence increased over sorts 1-4, plateauing at sort 4-5 (2.5-fold increase from sort 1 to 4, 3.9-fold vs bulk 15). Colonies from sorted populations had >5-fold higher fluorescence than bulk. Sequencing of top 30 colonies revealed 11 amino acid changes, 2 deletions, 9 silent mutations in AroY.B and AroY.Ciso. Eight variants were reconstructed in a clean background. Five showed significantly higher fluorescence; six showed higher CCM titers. Best variant AroY.B_P146T gave 13.7-fold CCM increase. PCA levels varied; some variants increased PCA, indicating incomplete conversion.
process_signals: Fluorescence plateau after sort 4
96% of sorted colonies had higher fluorescence than bulk
Mutations identified in both subunits
Reconstruction validated causality
Best variant: AroY.B_P146T (13.7-fold CCM increase)
Some variants increased PCA, suggesting incomplete conversion
methods: HPLC for CCM and PCA quantification
failures_or_limits: Some variants did not show significant fluorescence increase
Some variants increased PCA, indicating bottleneck not fully resolved

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: The study successfully merged OrthoRep and biosensors for evolving metabolic enzymes without growth advantage. Limitations: further engineering needed to reduce PCA secretion/diffusion; biosensor readout could be converted to growth-based selection for scalability. Future work: genome-wide adaptive evolution using growth-coupled biosensors.
process_signals: Success: evolution without growth coupling
Limitation: PCA secretion still an issue
Future direction: growth-coupled selection
failures_or_limits: Biosensor-based FACS may not scale easily

section_gists.heading: Fluorescence-assisted cell sorting
section_gists.gist: Detailed FACS protocol: Sc-105 cultured in Delft minimal medium, diluted to OD 0.5 in PBS, 1 million events analyzed, top 2% (10,000 cells) sorted into synthetic complete medium, recovered 24h, then transferred to Delft medium for another 24h before next sort. Bulk control passaged daily 1:5 dilution. Experiment done in triplicates.
process_signals: Sort gate: top 2%
Recovery steps between sorts
Bulk control: 1:5 dilution daily

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The initial hypothesis was that combining OrthoRep continuous mutagenesis with a CCM biosensor and FACS would enable rapid directed evolution of PCA decarboxylase without growth coupling.
The experimental design assumed that sorting the top 2% fluorescent cells each round would enrich for variants with improved PCA decarboxylase activity, leading to higher CCM production.
The plateau in fluorescence after sort 4 suggested that the selection pressure or mutation supply may have reached a limit, possibly due to biosensor saturation or other bottlenecks.
The reconstruction of individual variants confirmed that the observed fluorescence increases were due to mutations in AroY.B and AroY.Ciso, not other genomic changes.
The increase in PCA in some improved variants indicated that the PCA decarboxylase step was not the only bottleneck; PCA secretion or other factors may limit CCM yield.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Why did fluorescence plateau after sort 4? Was it due to biosensor saturation, mutation supply exhaustion, or other factors?
What is the mechanism by which the AroY.B_P146T mutation improves activity?
Can the workflow be applied to other enzymes or pathways?
How does the mutation spectrum of OrthoRep affect the types of variants obtained?
Would a growth-coupled selection be more effective for further improvements?

events.summary: PCA accumulation limits CCM production due to rate-limiting PCA decarboxylase.
Combining OrthoRep continuous mutagenesis with a CCM biosensor and FACS can rapidly evolve PCA decarboxylase without growth coupling.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers introduction, parental strain design, and selection process, providing key problem, hypothesis, design, and experimental events.

events.summary: Five iterative rounds of OrthoRep mutagenesis and FACS selection performed, sorting top 2% fluorescent cells each round.
Control experiment: 15 bulk passages without FACS selection to monitor drift.
Mutation rate and gene length used to estimate ~1 additional mutation per cell per sort round.
Population fluorescence increased 2.5-fold from sort 1 to 4, plateauing at sort 4-5; 3.9-fold vs bulk 15.
96% of colonies from sorted populations had higher fluorescence than bulk; sequencing revealed 11 amino acid changes.
Reconstructed variants validated: AroY.B_P146T gave 13.7-fold CCM increase and 77% PCA reduction.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed experimental design, selection process, validation of variants, and a noted limitation of the biosensor.

events.summary: The study acknowledges that further engineering is needed to limit PCA secretion or passive diffusion across the cellular membrane.
Future direction proposed: moving from directed evolution of predefined target genes to genome-wide adaptive laboratory evolution by coupling biosensor readout to growth.
Suggestion to diversify selection criteria beyond the stringent one used, to enable mutational drifting and adaptation of robust proteins.
The study notes a relatively low number of mutations observed per evolved PCA decarboxylase variant and a high false-discovery rate (>9/22, >0.45).
The study is presented as a first demonstration of rapid evolution of metabolic enzymes without direct fitness advantage using continuous hypermutation.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains discussion of limitations, future directions, and validation of the approach, which are key process events.
This chunk contains only acknowledgements, conflict of interest, author contributions, references, and supporting information. No research evolution events are described.

evidence_bank.quote: the build-up and secretion of the CCM pathway intermediate PCA remain rate-limiting for high CCM production

we combine the power of targeted in vivo mutagenesis using OrthoRep with high-throughput biosensing for the rapid evolution of rate-limiting metabolic reactions of the cis,cis-muconic acid (CCM) pathway
we coupled continuous mutagenesis using OrthoRep with selection using the BenM-based biosensor output as a proxy for CCM accumulation respectively.
We iterated culturing and FACS five times, where each sort collected 10 000 events
As a control evolution experiment, we cultured the parental starting strain over 15 consecutive 24 h cultivations without FACS selection by bulk passaging 20% of cells between each cultivation.
the actual mutation-rate per cell approximates $1 \times 10^{-4}$ substitutions per base.
the population-level fluorescence outputs increased following each round of sorting from the first to the fourth sort, reaching a total increase of 2.5-fold.
a maximum of 3.9-fold increase was observed when comparing mean fluorescence outputs following five rounds of sorting versus fifteen rounds of neutral drifting
more than 96% (309/319) of the colonies from sorted populations ('Sort4 + 5') had cells with higher fluorescence than cells from colonies undergoing neutral drifting
we found 11 amino acid changes, two deletion and nine silent mutations
KpAroY. B\_P146T performing the best with a 13.7-fold higher CCM titer
KpAroY. B\_P146T also showed significantly reduced PCA titers (77%, P < 0.01)
One strategy to consider further is the need to limit the secretion or passive diffusion, of PCA across the cellular membrane as is often observed in yeast engineered to produce PCA-derived chemicals
a logical next step could be to move from in vivo directed evolution of predefined target genes towards genome-wide adaptive laboratory evolution
transferring the biosensor read-out from fluorescence to growth would be beneficial from a technical and scalability point of view
diversify the selection criteria beyond the stringent one used in this study
tuning the selection strength during continuous evolution regimes has previously been demonstrated to enable mutational drifting and adaptation of robust proteins
increase the relatively low number of mutations observed per evolved PCA decarboxylase variant
limit the false-discovery rate observed in this study (>9/22, >0.45)
our study serves as a first demonstration of rapid evolution of metabolic enzymes without any direct fitness advantage using continuous hypermutation

event_graph.paper_main_problem: PCA accumulation limits CCM production due to rate-limiting PCA decarboxylase.

canonical_events.summary: PCA accumulation limits CCM production due to rate-limiting PCA decarboxylase.
Combining OrthoRep continuous mutagenesis with a CCM biosensor and FACS can rapidly evolve PCA decarboxylase without growth coupling.
Parental strain Sc-105 engineered with CCM pathway, BenM biosensor, and OrthoRep targeting AroY.B and AroY.Ciso on p1 plasmid.
Mutation rate (~1e-4 per base) and gene length (2.1 kb) used to estimate ~1 additional mutation per cell per sort round, guiding experimental design.
Five iterative rounds of OrthoRep mutagenesis and FACS selection, sorting top 2% fluorescent cells each round.
Control experiment: 15 bulk passages without FACS selection to monitor drift.
Population fluorescence increased 2.5-fold from sort 1 to 4, plateauing at sort 4-5; 3.9-fold vs bulk 15.
96% of colonies from sorted populations had higher fluorescence than bulk; sequencing revealed 11 amino acid changes in AroY.B and AroY.Ciso.
Reconstructed variants validated: AroY.B_P146T gave 13.7-fold CCM increase and 77% PCA reduction.
Fluorescence plateau after sort 4 suggests selection limit; some variants did not show significant fluorescence increase.
High false-discovery rate (>0.45) and low number of mutations per variant observed.
PCA secretion/diffusion remains a bottleneck; further engineering needed to limit PCA loss.
Future direction: move to genome-wide adaptive evolution by coupling biosensor readout to growth for scalability.
First demonstration of rapid evolution of metabolic enzymes without direct fitness advantage using continuous hypermutation.

edges.reason: The problem of PCA accumulation motivates the hypothesis to evolve PCA decarboxylase.
The hypothesis requires a parental strain with OrthoRep and biosensor.
Strain design allows mutation rate estimation to plan selection rounds.
Mutation rate estimate guides the iterative FACS selection experiment.
Control experiment runs in parallel to selection experiment.
FACS rounds produce population fluorescence data for analysis.
Bulk control provides baseline for fluorescence comparison.
Fluorescence increase leads to colony screening and sequencing.
Identified mutations are reconstructed to validate CCM improvement.
Sequencing reveals low mutation count and high false-discovery rate.
Despite CCM increase, PCA secretion remains a limitation.
PCA secretion issue suggests future growth-coupled selection.
Successful evolution validates the overall approach.
